story:
I am the only one of us who calls it rape. Stheno, when she must mention it, says “our bad luck;” Medusa shrugs and says “gods don’t have to ask.” I say but they should and she says but they don’t and Stheno says this attitude doesn’t help. She’s right. I go into my cave and scream.

It was not like with a man. The gods are called beautiful, but it is awe, not lust, they inspire. Poseidon’s embrace—if I must call it that—was like being swept away by the sea. Water, salt, foam. Fear. And afterward, pain. Worse still was the judgment of his proud-eyed niece, who said we had profaned her temple with our lust. Our lust. She punished us for surviving what her uncle chose to do.

Athena changed us more than he had, inside and out: snakes for hair, bronze hands, a gaze that turns the living to stone. We learned about the last gift when our mother came to see what kept us at the temple. One look. Silence. Rock.

We stayed there until the townspeople drove us out with fire—its glare dazed us, and we could not turn our fatal look on them all. So now we live in three caves on the hill. Theirs open inland, toward fields and smoke and the safe lives of others. Mine faces the sea. At night, when I hear the surf and think he may be out on the prowl, I bellow. A warning. To ships. To the water. To myself.

When I could not stand it anymore, my rage hardened into an idea. I wove a veil, packed a few apples in a bag—we do our best to live like people up here—and slipped out after dark.

You can see Olympus from anywhere in Thessaly. It glows a little at night, lit by reflected divinity, and so you can walk by night if you wish. After a few days I reached its foot and began to climb.

It is slow work. Moving quietly seems wisest, and my metal hands, though the stones do not harm them, clank. With each step, I feel myself drawing away from the mortal world, where gods lace us into the traps of their whim, and closer to the plane where they live, unguarded. With each step I carry the weight of our mother’s silence, the hiss of our hair, the word none of them will say.

When I reach their splendid halls, I will call out. I will ask the haughty virgin—punisher of victims—why she chose to protect her temple and not the girls dragged across its floor. I will tear aside my veil. And I will look her in the eye.

Not to spread harm for its own sake, but because she must finally see. Because if she will not admit what was done, then she can learn what it means to be stopped, to be answered, to be held to the cost of her justice. I do not wish to be a monster. But they made us this, and I will not pretend it was our bad luck, or that gods do not have to ask.
